attributes.stem:
A 75-year-old man presents to hospital with stage IV lung cancer with widespread metastases to his spine, pelvis, liver and brain. He was diagnosed with metastatic lung cancer eight months ago in which he declined any chemo-radiotherapy. He complains that he has increasing difficulty managing his pain and is in discomfort.

He is unable to sit upright in bed due to pain in his pelvis, in which he finds difficulty in eating and drinking. He has avoided solid foods since his pelvic pain as he fears choking on his food. He has lost 2kg in the past few days from the lack of oral intake.

He is currently on regular slow-release morphine with rapid release morphine PRN (total morphine use in the past 24 hours 32mg). He also takes paracetamol and ondansetron PRN.

On examination, he is lying supine on his bed and appears cachectic and in pain. All vital signs are within normal limits except a heart rate of 110/min. The patient has sunken eyes, loss of skin turgor and dry mucous membranes. There are no abnormalities on cardiopulmonary examination. There is tenderness on palpation of the lower back and pelvis.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Switch to a transdermal fentanyl patch
Switch oral morphine to morphine sachet (in suspension)
Use continuous subcutaneous infusion of morphine
attributes.explanation:
Switching the patient to a continuous subcutaneous infusion of morphine is a safe temporary alternative to work out the patient's analgesia requirement / best analgesia method before switching to other options e.g. transdermal patches. In this case, the patient most likely requires an admission given his poor control of pain symptoms. An interim continuous subcutaneous infusion pump can be given to work out analgesia requirements first before switching to other options more manageable in an outpatient setting.

Switching to a transdermal fentanyl patch may be a useful non-oral alternative. Fentanyl is a synthetic opioid that can be used in the treatment of chronic pain and the transdermal patch provides a slow steady release of analgesia into the blood. Fentanyl patches take 24–72 hours to reach maximum effect and steady-state concentration may not be reached until the second patch is applied. By switching oral morphine to a fentanyl patch immediately, the interim pain between the switch may not be covered.

Prescribing a benzodiazepine is indicated in end-of-life care to manage behavioural symptoms such as anxiousness and restlessness. However, Benzodiazepines does not provide pain relief and may increase the risk of severe respiratory depression.

Switching oral morphine to morphine suspension might help with administration, but it doesn't address the underlying issue of the patient's difficulty with oral intake due to pain when sitting upright. A parenteral route of administration would be more appropriate in this case.

Commencing chemoradiotherapy can have a role in pain relief in metastatic disease where the pain is confined to a localised site. However that is not the case here, as this patient has late-stage cancer with widespread metastases that are undergoing palliative treatment and this approach is unlikely to provide immediate or adequate pain relief.
learningPoints: A continuous PCA infusion is a safe temporary alternative to evaluate a patient's 24-hour analgesia requirement before switching to another pain regimen.
references: Tassinari D, Sartori S, Tamburini E, et al. Transdermal fentanyl as a front-line approach to moderate-severe pain: a meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials. J Palliat Care 2009; 25:172.
Hadley G, Derry S, Moore RA, Wiffen PJ. Transdermal fentanyl for cancer pain. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2013; :CD010270.
